title: Playbook - Microsoft Security To SharePoint To Power BI Reporting
applies_to: End-to-end reporting pipelines that combine Microsoft security data, SharePoint staging, and Power BI reporting
prerequisites: Decision on which Microsoft security surfaces provide the required metrics
SharePoint target lists or document libraries designed for staging
Power BI reporting artifact strategy defined
Logic Apps or equivalent orchestration path selected
required_permissions: Microsoft Graph permissions for attack simulation or other Graph security resources
Defender for Endpoint permissions for machine, vulnerability, or score APIs
SharePoint write permissions through Graph or the SharePoint connector
Power BI permissions for report consumption or REST-based provisioning when automation is needed
request_sequence: Split the data requirements by vendor surface rather than assuming one Microsoft security API covers everything.
For phishing simulation reporting, retrieve data from Microsoft Graph attackSimulation APIs.
For onboarded-device counts or device inventory, retrieve data from Microsoft Defender for Endpoint APIs such as /api/machines.
Normalize each feed into a staging schema with explicit extraction timestamps and business observation dates.
Store normalized rows in SharePoint lists for tabular metrics or SharePoint files for exported payloads and snapshots.
Build Power BI semantic models and reports on top of the SharePoint staging layer, or automate report lifecycle through the Power BI REST API if full provisioning is required.
Refresh Power BI on the cadence required by the reporting audience and monitor refresh history.
implementation_notes: This is the recommended integration shape when the goal is cross-surface Microsoft security reporting and the organization already uses SharePoint and Power BI.
Use SharePoint as the decoupling layer when the reporting logic needs stable snapshots, simplified Power BI ingestion, or a low-friction operational handoff.
For device counts by month, store periodic snapshots because live inventory APIs are current-state only.
For attack simulation, store both summary metrics and simulation-level detail as needed; the report audience determines whether you need one row per simulation or one row per user result.
caveats: There is no single Microsoft security API that cleanly returns both Defender for Endpoint device counts and phishing simulation results.
Direct Power BI provisioning is a separate concern from data extraction and staging; use the REST automation layer only when the project truly needs it.
Historical reporting requires persisted snapshots or staged period extracts even when the source APIs are rich.
